The Grasshopper is a 1970 drama film directed by Jerry Paris. It stars Jacqueline Bisset, Jim Brown, Joseph Cotten and Christopher Stone. Penny Marshall appears in a small role.

Plot
Christine Adams, a cheerful 19-year-old from British Columbia, Canada, travels to Los Angeles to be with her fiance, who works there in a bank. When the relationship doesn't work out, she moves to Las Vegas.

She finds work as a showgirl and meets Tommy Marcott, an African-American former pro football player who holds an executive position at the casino, though in truth he is only used as a "celebrity greeter." They fall in love and get married, but when Tommy gives a severe beating to a wealthy casino patron who had beaten and raped Christine, they flee Vegas, discussing the possibility that the casino patron will seek revenge. In Los Angeles, Tommy is unable to get a good job and his and Christine's relationship suffers, and then Tommy is shot dead on a basketball court, likely the anticipated act of revenge. 

After the funeral, Christine has a bad reaction to illicit drugs she consumes in her grief. She returns to Las Vegas and finds work as a V.I.P. "party girl". In that capacity she meets and is persuaded by wealthy client Richard Morgan to return to Los Angeles and be his mistress.

Christine is fond of Richard but she gets bored in her new life, so she becomes romantically involved with Jay Rigney, who she had previously known platonically. She persuades Jay she can get enough money that they can buy a ranch together. However that plan is doomed when Richard asks Christine to marry him, wanting her to spend all her time with him.

Jay persuades Christine the only way they can keep the dream of getting a ranch alive is if she becomes a prostitute and he works as her pimp. She ends her relationship with Richard and for a while things go as they planned. However one night she returns to her and Jay’s apartment to discover he has left her and taken all their money.

Christine goes to the airport where Richard’s private plane is kept, and, with a promise of “some fun” and by sharing a marijuana joint, induces airfield employee Elroy, who she had previously flirted with, to take her up in a skywriting plane. Still sharing the joint, Christine has Elroy write "FUCK IT" across the sky, to the amusement or consternation of those below. The police take away Christine and Elroy when they land. While being arrested Christine is asked her age, to which she replies, “twenty-two”.

RAAF Air Command is the operational headquarters of the Royal Australian Air Force, responsible for planning, directing, and coordinating all air and space operations in support of Australia's national security objectives. The current Commander of RAAF Air Command is Air Vice Marshal Catherine Roberts. The organization comprises a number of units, including the Air Operations Group, which manages air traffic control, base operations, and air defense; the Surveillance and Response Group, which operates the RAAF's fleet of maritime patrol aircraft and air defense radars; and the Air Combat Group, responsible for fighter aircraft and air combat operations. RAAF Air Command also has a significant role in supporting humanitarian and disaster relief efforts, both domestically and internationally. In recent years, Air Command has provided support to disaster relief operations in countries such as Indonesia, Papua New Guinea, and Vanuatu. The history of Air Command can be traced back to the formation of the RAAF in 1921. Over the years, its role has evolved to reflect changes in the nature of air power and the strategic environment. During World War II, Air Command played a crucial role in the defense of Australia and its territories, as well as in the Allied air campaigns in the Pacific. Today, RAAF Air Command continues to be at the forefront of Australian air power, with a focus on building capability and capacity to meet emerging threats and challenges. The organization is also committed to maintaining strong partnerships and alliances with other air forces and international organizations, and to promoting regional security and stability.

Aleksandr Antonovich Troyanovsky (Russian: Алекса́ндр Анто́нович Трояно́вский; 1 (13) January  1882, Tula – 23 June 1955, Moscow) was a Russian revolutionary, military officer and Soviet diplomat who served as the first authorized Soviet Ambassador to the United States.

Early life and revolutionary career 
Troyanovsky was born in the family of a hereditary nobleman and brigadier commander in the Imperial Russian Army. His father died from tuberculosis when he was a child. He and his younger brother joined the military and after graduating from the Mikhailovsky Artillery School he was sent to serve in the 33rd artillery brigade of the Kiev Military District.

From 1904 he was a member of the Russian Social Democratic Labour Party's Bolshevik faction and spread revolutionary and anti war propaganda during the Russo-Japanese War.

In April 1905, he  was sent to an artillery unit that took part in battles with Japanese troops in Manchuria. He resigned as a lieutenant in 1906, and in 1907 by the decision of the Kiev military district court he was deprived of all the rights of a retired officer.

In 1908 he was arrested for revolutionary activity, and on 24 February 1909 was sentenced by the Kiev District Court to administrative deportation to the Yenisei province. He served exile in the village of Tikhanovo, Belskaya volost, Yenisei district.

In 1910 he  escaped from exile and emigrated abroad with forged documents. He collaborated with the Bolsheviks. A member of the 9th Congress of the Second International in 1911 and the Poroninsky meetings of the Central Committee of the RSDLP with other party cadres. He was a member of the foreign editorial board of the journal "Education". He lived in France and Austria during exile, collaborated with V. I. Lenin and met with Joseph Stalin.

He broke with the Bolsheviks in 1914, became a Menshevik and maintained defencist positions.

In 1917 he returned to Russia, joined the Imperial Army, took part in hostilities on the Southwestern Front.

He was elected on the Menshevik list as a deputy to the Constituent Assembly, and spoke in favor of forming a bloc with the Socialist-Revolutionaries and Bolsheviks in the elections. At the only meeting of the Constituent Assembly on 5 January 1918, he spoke on the issue of concluding peace, while transparently hinting that the Bolsheviks were selling Russian interests in the war for "German gold".

At the Moscow party conference in May 1918, he was elected a member of the Central Committee of the Menshevik Party

After the outbreak of the Russian Civil War, from 1918 to 1921 he served in the Red Army and was a teacher at the School of Senior Instructors.

In July 1918 he was arrested for anti-Soviet activities and was held in the Butyrka Prison fountil his release in October. He was arrested for the second time in September 1920, and was held in prison for one month.

In the spring of 1920, when discussing Yuli Martov's report The Dictatorship of the Proletariat and Democracy, Troyanovsky drew attention to "the need for a more detailed answer to the question" and about the relationship between bourgeois democracy and proletarian democracy" there are no grounds for it", and then it should be admitted "that the only correct path to socialism is the path of Lenin."

From 1921 he was in the apparatus of the People's Commissariat of the Workers' and Peasants' Inspection of the RSFSR, and in 1923 he officially joined the Russian Communist Party (b).

From 1924 to 1927 he was chairman of the Board of the Gostorg of the RSFSR and a member of the board of the People's Commissariat of Foreign Trade of the USSR.

Soviet diplomat 
From 14 November 1927 to 24 January 1933 he was the Plenipotentiary Representative of the USSR in Japan.

In 1933 he was deputy Chairman of the State Planning Committee under the Council of People's Commissars of the USSR.

From 20 November 1933 to 1 October 1938 Troyanovsky served as the Plenipotentiary Representative of the USSR in the United States. The presentation of credentials to U.S. President Franklin D. Roosevelt took place not two weeks later which was the usual procedure, but the day after Troyanovsky's arrival in Washington, which was evidence of the desire of the U.S. president to emphasize special respect and attention to the country. Welcoming Troyanovsky, Roosevelt expressed his satisfaction at the appointment to the post of Soviet ambassador to Washington "a man known for his friendly attitude towards the USA". The years of Troyanovsky's tenure as plenipotentiary representative of the USSR were years of formation of Soviet-American relations and the first steps towards cooperation in the trade, economic, scientific and cultural fields. Troyanovsky played an important role in the conclusion of the 1935 Soviet-American trade agreement, which was renewed annually in subsequent years. Roosevelt once joked at one of the receptions in the White House: "If Mr. Troyanovsky says, looking at the moon, that this is the sun, I personally, gentlemen, will believe him...".

He had direct access to Stalin, to whom he reported on the strengths of American life that were considered to be adopted by the USSR. In his reports from Washington he advocated rapprochement with the United States in the face of the threat of Nazism.

Later life 
From 1941 he taught at the Higher Diplomatic School of the People's Commissariat for Foreign Affairs of the Soviet Union. From 1941 worked in the Soviet Information Bureau under the Council of Ministers of the USSR. From 1947 he was a professor at the Higher Diplomatic School.

He was a member of the Central Executive Committee of the Soviet Union.

Troyanovsky died on 23 June 1955, at the age of 73. He was buried at the Novodevichy Cemetery in Moscow. His son Oleg also became a Soviet diplomat and Soviet Union's permanent representative to the United Nations.

Jiřina Šejbalová (17 September 1905- 23 August 1981) was a Czech actress of the stage, television, and film. For many years she taught theatre classes at the Janáček Academy of Music and Performing Arts.

Born in Prague, Šejbalová came from a family of clerks. After studying at the Prague Conservatory, she began her career as an opera singer at the National Theatre in Brno in the mid-1920s. During the 1927-1928 season she abandoned her opera career and began working as a dramatic actress at that house in avant-garde plays directed by Jiří Frejka. She became a member of the National Theatre in Prague in 1928. She performed as a stage actress at that theatre up until her retirement in 1971.

Šejbalová made her first foray into Czech film as the title character in the 1929 movie Hanka a Jindra. She appeared in over 35 Czech feature films over the next five decades, with her last appearance being in the 1978 movie Sólo pro starou dámu. Her two most important films were Morálka paní Dulské (1958) and Zlatá svatba (1972). She also appeared is several Czech television movies and television programs from 1961 through 1980. She died in Prague at the age of 75.

Kabir Mohabbat is an Indian fashion designer known for his modern and contemporary approach to Indian ethnic wear. Born and raised in Delhi, Kabir developed a passion for fashion at an early age and pursued a career in design after completing his education in fashion design from a reputed institute in India. Kabir Mohabbat started his career in fashion by working with a number of established designers in India. He garnered experience and skills that helped him set his own style and design philosophy. Kabir believes in simplicity and elegance and designs clothes that are modern yet rooted in tradition. In 2012, Kabir launched his eponymous label "Kabir Mohabbat" which quickly caught the attention of the fashion industry. His first collection was a fusion of Indian and western wear and received rave reviews for its unique and fresh designs. Kabir's collection showcased traditional fabrics, like silk and cotton, in modern styles, making them appealing to a younger generation. Since then, Kabir Mohabbat has showcased his collections at various fashion weeks in India and abroad. His designs are known for their intricate embroidery and use of vibrant colors that blend seamlessly to create visual masterpieces. Kabir's signature style is a blend of traditional and contemporary designs, making his clothes versatile and suitable for all occasions. Kabir Mohabbat has designed for a range of clients, from Bollywood celebrities to fashion-conscious individuals. His work has also been featured in various fashion magazines and his label has become a recognizable brand in the Indian fashion industry. In conclusion, Kabir Mohabbat is a rising star in the Indian fashion industry known for his contemporary approach to Indian ethnic wear. His collections showcase his design philosophy of simplicity and elegance, making his clothes appeal to a wider audience. Kabir Mohabbat has set a benchmark in the Indian fashion industry and is set to redefine Indian fashion in the years to come.

Ivan Panteleyevich Mozgovenko (; 13 February 1924 – 31 December 2021) was a Soviet and Russian clarinetist and music teacher. He taught clarinet as a professor at the Gnessin State Musical College. For his participation as a soldier in World War II and for his later artistic achievements, he received numerous medals and honorary titles, including the title People's Artist of Russia.

Biography 
Mozgovenko was born on 13 February 1924 in Yashalta, Stepnovsky District, Rostov Region, Russian SFSR, USSR (now the Republic of Kalmykia in the Russian Federation). In 1931, his family's property was expropriated and the family was exiled to the Ural region in the area of Nizhny Tagil. From 1939 to 1943, he studied at the Tchaikovsky Music College in Sverdlovsk in the clarinet class. In 1943, he joined the Ural Volunteer Tank Corps, where he served in the medical battalion, fighting from Oryol, Bryansk and Zhitomir to Berlin and Prague. He was offered a commission as his unit demobilized, but continued his musical studies instead.

In 1946, he continued his training as a clarinetist at the Gnessin State Musical College, where he studied with Alexander Leonidovich Shtark (or Stark) (first degree in 1951, the graduate school in 1954). In 1951 he won the first prize at an international competition in Berlin and since worked as a soloist and chamber musician. He played with the Borodin Quartet. From 1953 to 1968, Mozgovenko was principal clarinetist with the State Symphony Cinema Orchestra. In collaboration with the Borodin Quartet and other chamber ensembles, Mozgovenko recorded works by Mozart, Brahms, Prokofiev and some contemporary composers. In addition to his concert activities, Mozgovenko taught at the Gnessin State Musical College since 1951, and as an associate professor since 1966. In 1980 he was awarded the title of professor. His students include many clarinetists and award winners of international competitions. Mozgovenko is also the author of a number of textbooks and transcriptions for clarinet.

Mozgovenko performed with Russian quartets like the Prokofiev, Beethoven, Komitas and Glinka quartets, and with musicians like Svyatoslav Richter, Sergei Prokofiev, Maria Yudina, Van Cliburn and Mstislav Rostropovich.

He collaborated with notable conductors like Yevgeny Svetlanov, Kirill Kondrashin, Alexander Gauk, Leonard Bernstein, Yuri Temirkanov, Vladimir Fedoseyev and Gennady Rozhdestvensky. He trained many musicians, becoming a founder of the Russian clarinet school.

Mozgovenko died on 31 December 2021, at the age of 97.

Instruments 

When Mosgowenko began to play the clarinet, the German fingering system for the clarinet dominated in the USSR. In the early 1960s, there was a general changeover to the Boehm system (including the Reform Boehm system, which came closer to the German sound, until the 1980s), which Mozgovenko was unable to avoid. In 1964, he also changed and has been playing Buffet Crampon instruments for many years.

Significance 
In Russia, Mozgovenko was considered the father of the current generation of clarinetists. , fourteen full or associate professors and 50 prize winners were his students.
Mozgovenko said:
"I am glad that my students are working all over the world – in the USA, Canada and France. I have celebrated 70 years of teaching at the academy."

In a tribute to his 95th birthday it is reported that he saw himself as the "creative grandson" of the founder of the Russian clarinet school Sergej Sobnow, whom he regarded as the successor to the founder of the clarinet school in Germany Carl Baermann.

On his 90th birthday he performed again. Beginning on the occasion of his 95th birthday, an international clarinet competition named after Mozgovenko has been taking place in Russia since 2019.

For Margarita Konstantinovna, one of his former students, Mozgovenko is an icon of Russian and world-spiritual art, who has made a great contribution to its development, with its own school, its own technique and many students.

Recordings 
Mosgowenko has made a number of vinyl recordings since 1951. His main recordings are:

 Mozart's clarinet quintet KV 581 with the Borodin Quartet, with whom he worked from 1951 to 1976. The recording was made in 1961. The artist played a German clarinet at the time and was committed to German clarinet-playing tradition. The recording is still available on CD. 
 the clarinet quintet by Brahms op. 115, also with the Borodin Quartet, recorded in 1969 (with Böhm clarinet), available on CD. 
 the clarinet quintet by the contemporary Russian composer Alexander Lokshin, recorded in 1960 with the Komitas Quartet, available on CD. 
 Sergei Prokofjew, Overture on Hebrew themes op. 34, with Sviatoslav Richter, Piano, in: Sviatoslav Richter – Plays Rakhmaninov & Prokofiev, 11 CDs, CD Nr. 11/1  (another compilation: )

There are also a number of radio recordings.

Mnesia is a distributed database management system designed for use in the Erlang programming language. It was developed by Ericsson AB, a Swedish telecommunications company, and is used by various companies such as WhatsApp and RabbitMQ. Mnesia is designed for high availability, fault tolerance, and scalability. Mnesia is an acronym for "Memory Network System Architecture," and it provides a set of APIs for managing data storage and retrieval, including functions for creating, updating, and deleting data records. Mnesia also provides a powerful query language that enables users to extract data from the database. One of the main features of Mnesia is its ability to store data across multiple nodes, making it a distributed database system. This feature enables Mnesia to provide consistent data even in the event of node failures, which makes it highly resilient and fault-tolerant. Moreover, Mnesia offers real-time database replication and conflict handling, ensuring that data is always consistent across all nodes. Mnesia can be used in several kinds of applications, such as telecommunication, e-commerce, and social networking, where high scalability and robustness are requirements. Mnesia is known for its ability to handle massive amounts of data and high transaction rates without compromising performance. Mnesia offers a highly flexible and extensible architecture that allows users to customize data querying and storage algorithms for specific use cases. Furthermore, Mnesia supports different storage backends such as RAM, disk, and hybrid, giving users the flexibility to choose which storage option best suits their requirements. In conclusion, Mnesia is an efficient and reliable distributed database management system that offers high-availability, scalability, and fault tolerance. Its numerous features and flexible architecture make it an ideal choice for use in various applications, where the need is to maintain a consistent and strong data system.

Harriet Ward is a renowned British social worker and a professor of Child and Family Research at Loughborough University. She's best known for having developed the concept of ‘resilient therapy’, which seeks to support children involved in child protection processes to build relationships, feel safe and heal from harm. Ward completed her Bachelor's degree in Sociology and Social Policy at the University of Nottingham and her Masters and Ph.D. in Social Research at the University of London. With over 30 years of experience in child welfare, she has taken a leading role in the development and enhancement of child protection systems worldwide. Ward has written several books and papers on child protection practice, including her renowned book "Child Protection - An Introduction" which provides an overview of key issues, debates, policies, and practices in child protection. She has also conducted research on child welfare and the protection of children from abuse, neglect, and exploitation. Her work has focused on the development of new interventions, preventative approaches, and on improving the experience that vulnerable children and young people have when they receive help. Harriet has advised the British government, guiding relevant policy and practice, and has been presented with many awards, including the ‘outstanding contribution to research excellence’ and the ‘lifetime achievement’ awards by the prestigious National Children and Adult Services Conference. Additionally, she has been elected as a fellow of the Academy of Social Sciences, recognizing her significant contribution to social science policy and practice. Today, Ward continues to provide guidance and recommendations to governments and child welfare organizations across the globe to ensure that the rights and well-being of children remain at the heart of policy and practice. Her work has undoubtedly contributed significantly to the development of child welfare practice, the protection of vulnerable children, and advancing child welfare policies and programs.

10551 Göteborg, provisional designation , is a stony Eoan asteroid and slow rotator from the outer region of the asteroid belt, approximately 13 kilometers in diameter. It was discovered on 18 December 1992, by Belgian astronomer Eric Elst at CERGA in Caussols (), southeastern France. The asteroid was named after the Swedish city of Gothenburg (Göteborg).

Orbit and classification 

Göteborg is a member of the Eos family, an orbital group of more than 4,000 asteroids, which are well known for mostly being of stony composition with a relatively high albedo. It orbits the Sun in the outer main-belt at a distance of 2.8–3.2 AU once every 5 years and 2 months (1,892 days). Its orbit has an eccentricity of 0.06 and an inclination of 11° with respect to the ecliptic.

The body's observation arc begins 61 years prior to its official discovery observation, with a precovery taken the night before its first identification as  at Lowell Observatory in January 1931.

Physical characteristics

Slow rotator 

In September 2012, photometric observations of Göteborg at the Palomar Transient Factory, California, rendered a rotational lightcurve with a period of  hours, or 14 days, and a brightness variation of 0.70 magnitude (). This makes a slow rotator, as most asteroids of this size typically have much shorter rotation periods.

Diameter and albedo 

According to the survey carried out by the NEOWISE mission of NASA's Wide-field Infrared Survey Explorer, Göteborg measures 15.491 and 15.689 kilometers in diameter and its surface has an albedo of 0.084 and 0.1169, respectively The Collaborative Asteroid Lightcurve Link assumes an albedo of 0.14, taken from 221 Eos, the family's largest member and namesake – and calculates a diameter of 11.53 kilometers based on an absolute magnitude of 12.44.

Naming 

This minor planet was named after Gothenburg (Göteborg), Sweden's second-largest city and the largest port in the Nordic countries, located on the country's southwest coast. Founded in the early 17th century and heavily influenced by the Dutch, the city still has its typical canal system. Later, the Swedes acquired political power over Gothenburg and the city flourished with the development of the Swedish East India Company in the early 18th century. The approved naming citation was published by the Minor Planet Center on 20 March 2000 ().

Henry Kessler (1847 – January 9, 1900) was an American Major League Baseball player who played mainly shortstop for the Brooklyn Atlantics of the National Association and the Cincinnati Reds of the National League.

Kessler was born in Brooklyn, New York sometime in 1847. He made his National Association debut on August 4, , and played in his only game of the season on the date. He collected one hit in five at bats, also getting a run batted in. The Atlantics defeated the Elizabeth Resolutes 16–8 in that game. He played in 14 games in . On September 30, he broke his thumb while playing catcher. His team had to continue the game with only 8 players (their centerfielder came in to play catcher.) In spite of all this, the Atlantics actually won the game 9-8. Kessler finished the 1874 season with a .304 batting average and four runs batted in. In , Kessler played in 25 of the Atlantics' 44 games. He batted .248 with 26 hits in 105 at bats. The Atlantics went 2–42 in 1875, and Brooklyn and the National Association folded after the season.

After 1875, Kessler played for the Cincinnati Reds of the National League in both  and . He finished with a .246 batting average in his two seasons in Cincinnati, playing in 59 games in 1876 and 6 games in 1877. His 47 errors at shortstop in 1876 were the second most in the league. The Reds were losing teams in each of Kessler's two years, placing 8th in the National League standing in 1876, and 6th in 1877.

Kessler would not play in the majors after 1877.  He played for several minor league teams until the late 1880s. In , he played for both Franklin of the Iron & Oil Association and Hamilton in the Ohio State League. Kessler also played with the Columbus Senators in the Tri-State League in .

On January 9, 1900, Kressler died in Franklin, Pennsylvania at the age of 53. As of 2012, he is the only former major league player to die in Franklin. He was buried at County Poor Farm Cemetery in Sugar Creek, Pennsylvania.

JibJab is an American digital entertainment company that specializes in creating humorous animated content, including e-cards, videos, and GIFs. The company was founded in 1999 by brothers Gregg and Evan Spiridellis, with the goal of creating entertaining and shareable content that would bring people together through laughter. JibJab gained widespread popularity with their animated e-cards, which allowed users to upload photos of themselves and create personalized, humorous greetings for holidays and special occasions. Over the years, the company expanded their offerings to include political satire, music videos, and a variety of other humorous content. In 2004, JibJab gained national attention with the release of their animated music video "This Land," which parodied the presidential election between George W. Bush and John Kerry. The video received millions of views and became a viral sensation, solidifying JibJab as a major player in the digital entertainment space. Since then, JibJab has continued to produce creative and relevant content, with a focus on humor and social commentary. Their videos have tackled issues like the economy, healthcare, and social media, often using popular songs and well-known celebrities to convey their message. Today, JibJab remains a popular destination for audiences who are looking for a laugh and a way to connect with others through shared experiences. The company continues to innovate and release new content, reaching audiences across platforms like social media, messaging apps, and streaming services. Overall, JibJab has had a profound impact on the world of digital entertainment, bringing humor and levity to millions of people and demonstrating the power of laughter to connect communities and bring joy to people's lives.

Alexis Peterson is an American professional basketball player who currently plays as a guard for the Seattle Storm of the Women's National Basketball Association (WNBA). She was born on June 20, 1995, in Columbus, Ohio. Peterson played college basketball at Syracuse University, where she was an All-American and the Atlantic Coast Conference (ACC) Player of the Year during the 2016-2017 season. During her college career, Peterson played in 134 games for the Syracuse Orange and averaged 13.5 points, 3.2 rebounds, and 4.8 assists per game. She was instrumental in leading the Orange to their first NCAA Women's Basketball Tournament Final Four appearance in 2016, where they were defeated by the University of Connecticut. In her senior year, she led the nation in total assists with 250 and was named the ACC Player of the Year. After college, Peterson was selected 15th overall in the 2017 WNBA draft by the Seattle Storm. She played for the Storm during the 2017 season but was waived before the start of the 2018 season. Peterson then played overseas in Israel and Turkey before being re-signed by the Storm in 2020. In her first game back with the Storm, she scored 12 points and had 4 rebounds in a win against the New York Liberty. Outside of basketball, Peterson is known for her efforts in promoting social justice and racial equality. She has been active in speaking out against police brutality and advocating for equal opportunities for people of color. In 2020, she joined the WNBA's Social Justice Council, which aims to fight systemic racism and promote social justice initiatives. In conclusion, Alexis Peterson is a talented basketball player and an advocate for social justice. Her contributions to the sport of basketball and her work off the court have made her a role model for young athletes all over the world.

Dawachi is a small village located in the Western Province of Saudi Arabia. It is part of the Al Bahah Region and lies near the province's border with the Makkah Region. The village is situated at an elevation of 1,400 meters above sea level and is known for its scenic beauty, as it is surrounded by mountains and valleys. The population of Dawachi is estimated to be around 2,500 people, primarily made up of the indigenous Bani Amir tribe. The people of Dawachi earn their livelihood through agriculture and animal husbandry. The village is self-sufficient in terms of food production, with its residents producing wheat, dates, and vegetables. The village of Dawachi offers a glimpse into traditional and rural life in Saudi Arabia. The houses in Dawachi are made of local stones and mud, and many of them have been passed down through generations. The village also has a mosque, a school, and a small market. Dawachi is a popular tourist destination, especially during the summer months when the weather is cooler. Tourists come to Dawachi to hike through the nearby mountains, explore local farms, and enjoy the fresh air and natural surroundings. The village is also known for its traditional crafts, such as weaving and pottery, which are popular souvenirs for visitors. In recent years, the Saudi government has made efforts to develop rural tourism in areas such as Dawachi. The government has provided funding to improve infrastructure, such as roads and water systems, and has also encouraged local residents to establish small businesses, such as restaurants and souvenir shops. Overall, Dawachi offers a unique blend of natural beauty, traditional culture, and modern amenities, making it a must-visit destination for anyone looking to experience rural Saudi Arabia.

American Idols Live! Tour 2013 was a concert tour featuring the top ten finalists of the twelfth season of the popular American reality TV show, American Idol. The tour commenced on July 19, 2013, in Kent, Washington, and concluded on August 31, 2013, in Nashville, Tennessee. Throughout the 30 shows, the contestants performed cover versions of some of the famous songs, including their own songs from the show. The top 10 finalists of American Idol's season 12 who participated in the tour were Candice Glover, Kree Harrison, Angie Miller, Amber Holcomb, Lazaro Arbos, Janelle Arthur, Burnell Taylor, Paul Jolley, Devin Velez, and Curtis Finch Jr. The tour was hosted by season 11 finalist Aubrey Cleland. The setlist of American Idols Live! Tour 2013 consisted of some of the most popular songs from the previous seasons of the show, including "Proud Mary," "I Will Always Love You," and "I Don't Want to Miss a Thing." The finalists also performed original songs from the season, such as Candice Glover's "I Am Beautiful" and Kree Harrison's "All Cried Out." The tour was highly praised by critics and fans alike for the energy and enthusiasm of the contestants. The tour was a commercial success, and many shows were sold out. Overall, the American Idols Live! Tour 2013 was a huge success, and it gave fans a chance to see their favorite contestants perform live after their favorite show ended. In conclusion, the American Idols Live! Tour 2013 was an exceptional experience for the fans and the contestants as well. It showcased the incredible talent of the finalists of Season 12 of American Idol and proved that they had the potential to become music superstars. The tour was a culmination of talent, hard work, and dedication, and it will always be remembered as one of the most successful tours in the history of American Idol.

Ben Britton (born October 7, 1987) is an Australian musician and producer hailing from Byron Bay, New South Wales. He is best known for his work in the electronic music scene, blending genres such as house, techno, and bass together to create a unique sound. Early Life and Career Britton's musical career began at a young age, playing guitar in several bands throughout high school. He later attended the Queensland University of Technology to study sound engineering and music production. After completing his degree, he moved to Melbourne to pursue a career in music. In 2011, Britton co-founded the electronic music collective and record label, Unknown Records. The label quickly gained a following in Australia and beyond, releasing several successful EPs and albums, including Britton's own solo EP, Shifted. The label's ethos is to provide a platform for emerging artists, with a focus on pushing the boundaries of electronic music. Musical Style and Influences Britton's music is heavily influenced by his diverse musical background, with nods to genres such as house, techno, and bass. He often incorporates live instrumentation into his productions, such as guitar and drums, adding an organic element to his electronic compositions. He cites artists such as Radiohead, Daft Punk, and Trentemøller as major inspirations for his work. His live performances are known for their high energy, with Britton often incorporating live drums and guitar into his sets. Discography - Shifted (EP) - Unknown Records (2011) - Dream Sequence (EP) - Unknown Records (2013) - The Sun In My Eyes (Album) - Unknown Records (2015) - Drifter (EP) - Unknown Records (2017) - Sunshower (EP) - Unknown Records (2019) Personal Life Britton currently resides in Byron Bay, where he continues to produce and perform music. He has expressed a desire to continue pushing the boundaries of electronic music, experimenting with new sounds and styles. In his free time, he enjoys surfing and exploring the natural beauty of the Byron Bay area.

The Iowa Aviation Museum is a museum dedicated to the history and preservation of aviation in the state of Iowa and the United States. Located in Greenfield, Iowa, the museum boasts a collection of over 20 aircraft and aviation artifacts, as well as an extensive library of aviation publications and archives. The museum was established in 1983 by a group of aviation enthusiasts who recognized the need for a dedicated space to showcase the rich aviation history in Iowa. The museum is housed in a former Army Air Corps hangar, which adds to the historical significance of the collection. The museum's collection includes numerous vintage aircraft from different eras, including a 1928 Curtiss-Wright Travel Air 4000, a 1942 Stearman PT-17, and a 1956 Piper PA-22 Tri-Pacer. In addition to these aircraft, the museum also features a collection of aviation artifacts, including uniforms, pilot gear, engines, and instruments. One of the most significant exhibits at the Iowa Aviation Museum is the display dedicated to Iowa native and aviation pioneer, Clyde Cessna. Cessna is best known for founding the Cessna Aircraft Company, which manufactured hundreds of thousands of aircraft during its history. The exhibit showcases rare photographs and artifacts from Cessna's life, as well as his contributions to the aviation industry. The museum also offers a variety of educational programs and events, including summer camps, lectures, and workshops, that educate visitors on the history and science of aviation. The museum also features a flight simulator that allows visitors to experience the thrill of flying a plane. In conclusion, the Iowa Aviation Museum is an important institution that preserves and celebrates the rich aviation history of Iowa and the United States. With its impressive collection of vintage aircraft and aviation artifacts, as well as its commitment to education, the museum is a valuable resource for aviation enthusiasts and historians alike.

Saccopharynx lavenbergi, also known as the "black dragonfish," is a species of deep-sea fish that resides in the bathypelagic zone of the oceans. This species was first discovered in 1985 during a research expedition in the Pacific Ocean and was named in honor of the oceanographer Charles Lavenberg. The black dragonfish is part of the family Stomiidae, which includes several other species of deep-sea fish. They are among the most abundant predators in their habitat due to their unique adaptations to the harsh environment of the deep ocean. These adaptations include an elongated and slender body shape, which allows them to move quickly through the water and catch prey, and a large mouth with long, sharp fang-like teeth that can be angled backwards to trap their prey. Saccopharynx lavenbergi is a highly specialized predator that feeds on other deep-sea fish, including lanternfish, hatchetfish, and myctophids. They rely on their keen sense of smell and vision to locate their prey, as well as a long, worm-like light organ that extends from their chin. This light organ, which is called a photophore, produces red light and is used to lure in prey that are attracted to it. Despite their ferocious appearance, black dragonfish are relatively small in size, with females growing up to 30 centimeters in length and males reaching 12 centimeters. They have a dark brown or black coloration, which allows them to blend in with the dark water of their habitat. Due to their deep-sea habitat, black dragonfish are not commonly encountered by humans. However, they play an important role in the ecosystem as top predators and are studied by researchers to gain a better understanding of the ecology of the deep ocean.

Cuthbert Taylor (1870–1938) was a British electrical engineer and inventor who made significant contributions to the development of early radio and wireless communication technology. He is best known for his invention of the Taylor loop aerial, which greatly improved the efficiency of radio communication systems. Early Life and Education Cuthbert Taylor was born in Glasgow, Scotland in 1870. He attended Glasgow University, where he received a degree in electrical engineering. After completing his studies, Taylor worked as an engineer for several companies, including the British Post Office and the Marconi Company. Inventions and Contributions Taylor is best known for his invention of the Taylor loop aerial, which he patented in 1906. The loop antenna greatly improved the efficiency and range of radio communication systems, particularly for medium and long-wave frequencies. The invention was quickly adopted by the military and commercial wireless communication industries and became widely used throughout the world. In addition to his work on the loop aerial, Taylor also made important contributions to the development of radio direction finding systems and radio navigation. During World War I, he worked closely with the British military to develop advanced wireless communication systems for naval and aerial operations. Later Life and Legacy Taylor continued to work in the field of electrical engineering throughout his life, making significant contributions to the development of radio and wireless communication technology. He died in 1938 at the age of 68. Today, Taylor's legacy is remembered through his pioneering work in the field of wireless communication. The Taylor loop aerial remains an important technology in radio communication systems, and the principles behind it continue to be used in modern antenna designs.

"Glory" is a song by American rapper Common and American singer John Legend. It was written by John Legend, Common, and Rhymefest. The song was released on December 11, 2014, by Columbia Records as the theme song from the 2014 film Selma, which portrays the 1965 Selma to Montgomery marches. Common also co-starred in the film as Civil Rights Movement leader James Bevel.

Commercially, the song peaked at number 49 on the US Billboard Hot 100. A music video for the song was directed by Paramount Pictures and was released on January 12, 2015. The song won the award for Best Original Song at the 87th Academy Awards (2015) and the 72nd Golden Globe Awards (2015), as well as the award for Best Song Written for Visual Media at the 58th Annual Grammy Awards (2016).

Awards

Music video

The music video was directed by Paramount Pictures. It was put on Common's official Vevo account on January 12, 2015. The video stars Legend and Common. The video, which includes footage from the film, begins with Legend playing the piano and then singing, with Common rapping in a different location.

Live performances
"Glory" was performed by Legend and Common on the American morning television show Good Morning America on January 5, 2015.

On February 8, 2015, the duo performed the song as culmination to the 57th Annual Grammy Awards ceremonies held at the Staples Center in Los Angeles, California.

Legend and Common performed "Glory" during the 87th Academy Awards show on February 22, 2015 using a stage-setting of Selma's Edmund Pettus Bridge. The song won the Oscar for Best Original Song.

In October 2015, Common surprised a student choir in Compton, California with a live performance of "Glory" in support of My Brother's Keeper Challenge. The promotion was run by Omaze, a private internet company that raises funds for charity through online auctions and merchandise sales.

It was performed by Legend and Common, backed by a choir, at the 2020 Democratic National Convention. They also played it during a Joe Biden rally at Pennsylvania, a day before the 2020 United States presidential election.

Commercial performance
"Glory" debuted on the Billboard Hot 100 at number 92. It became Common's fourth charted song of his own, and peaked at number 49.

Springside is a historic estate and park located in Poughkeepsie, New York. The estate dates back to the early 19th century and was once the home of Matthew Vassar, the founder of Vassar College. History The Springside estate was established in 1802 by successful businessman John Howard. In 1824, it was purchased by Matthew Vassar, who transformed the property into a lavish estate. Vassar added new buildings, gardens, and a lake, and also constructed a mansion on the property. The mansion was designed in the Italianate style by architect Sidney Stratton, and was completed in 1851. After Vassar's death in 1868, the estate remained in the family until 1910, when it was sold to William F. Havemeyer. Havemeyer used the property as a summer home until his death in 1924. In 1925, the estate was donated to the city of Poughkeepsie to be used as a public park. Features Springside Park covers over 200 acres and features a variety of attractions, including hiking trails, picnic areas, and a fishing lake. The Mansion serves as the park's visitor center and is open to the public for tours. The park also has an outdoor amphitheater which hosts numerous concerts and events throughout the year. The park is home to Springside National Historic Landmark, which recognizes the estate's historical and cultural significance. The landmark designation also helps to preserve the property for future generations to enjoy. Today, Springside Park remains a popular destination for visitors and locals alike. Its natural beauty and rich history make it a valuable community asset and a treasured part of Poughkeepsie's heritage.

Stekene () is a municipality located in the Belgian province of East Flanders. Since 1976, the municipality has comprised the towns of Kemzeke, Klein-Sinaai and Stekene proper. It also includes the village Koewacht. On July 1, 2007, Stekene had a total population of 17,040. The total area is 44.80 km² which results in a population density of 376 inhabitants per km² at that time. The population at the start of 2018 was 
18,352.
Stekene profiles itself as an oasis of green within industrialized Flanders as a large part of its area consists of forests and agricultural lands. The E34 road, from Antwerp to the coast, passes through the municipality and also has its own access to it. Another part of the community is the N403, which connects to Sint-Niklaas.

Geography

The municipality of Stekene is located in the region of Waasland, on the border with the Netherlands, 10 km north-west of Sint-Niklaas and 20 km west of Antwerp. Stekene borders Sint-Gillis-Waas in the east, Sint-Niklaas to the south, Moerbeke to the west and the Dutch town of Hulst to the north. It is  distant from Antwerp.

was a Japanese animator who worked with Toei Animation, TMS Entertainment, and Studio Ghibli. He was considered to be one of Japan's foremost animators, and he was an important mentor to both Hayao Miyazaki and Isao Takahata.

Biography
Ōtsuka was born in Shimane Prefecture. During a visit to Tsuwano at the age of 10, he saw a steam locomotive for the first time. He began to take an interest in locomotives and their operation and would frequently sketch them. In 1945, his family moved to Yamaguchi Prefecture where there was a military base. He began to draw the array of military vehicles located there while also drawing a variety of subjects in different styles. Otsuka also collected cuttings of cartoon strips into scrapbooks to learn more about drawing styles. Otsuka joined Yamaguchi's Bureau of Statistics but later wanted to become a political cartoonist in Tokyo. However at the time, permission was needed to move to Tokyo so he applied to the Health and Welfare Ministry. After passing the exam, he was assigned to the drug enforcement division as an assistant who maintained the firearms of the agents. Around this time he suffered from tuberculosis.

Career
In 1956, Otsuka saw an advertisement in the Yomiuri Shinbun where Toei was soliciting job applications for animators. After passing the test, Otsuka worked with Yasuji Mori and Akira Daikubara on The Tale of the White Serpent and learnt their approaches. Wanting to learn more animation theory, he began to seek out textbooks and was shown a textbook on US animation written by Preston Blair. After working on Magic Boy in 1959, his animation of a skeleton was unintentionally considered comical due to its realism. This led to comical bad guy characters becoming Otsuka's specialty. He came to believe that genuine realism doesn't suit animation and "constructed realism" is more suitable. Hayao Miyazaki compared Otsuka to Kenichi Enomoto in the use of this approach. After completion of his next film, The Wonderful World of Puss 'n Boots, Otsuka left Toei to join A Production.

Otsuka has written several books about the anime industry. His  is considered "a prime resource for the history of 1960s and 1970s anime".

In July 2002, an exhibition of his work and personal pieces was held in Ginza.

From 2003 until his death, Otsuka was an advisor to Telecom Animation Film.

Demographics of New Brunswick New Brunswick is a Canadian province situated in the eastern part of the country. As of 2021, the province's population was estimated to be 781,400 people, making it the eighth-most populous province in Canada. The province's population is concentrated in its urban centers, with more than 60 percent of residents living in metropolitan areas. Ethnicity and Culture New Brunswick has a diverse cultural and ethnic makeup. The largest ethnic group in the province is English, followed by French, Scottish, Irish, and German. The province has a significant indigenous population, and several First Nations communities are located throughout the province. Language The official language of New Brunswick is English, and it is the primary language spoken by the majority of the population. The province is also home to a significant Francophone population, with more than 30 percent of the population speaking French as their first language. Religion New Brunswick has a diverse religious landscape, with Christianity being the most widely practiced religion. The province has significant communities of Roman Catholics, Baptists, Anglicans, and United Church members, among others. The province is home to a small Muslim community, as well as several Jewish congregations. Education New Brunswick has a robust education system, with several universities, community colleges, and technical schools located throughout the province. The province is home to the University of New Brunswick, Mount Allison University, and St. Thomas University, among others. Economy New Brunswick has a diverse economy that includes several industries, including agriculture, manufacturing, and forestry. The province's largest employers include the provincial government, healthcare, the retail sector, and education. Conclusion New Brunswick is a diverse and culturally rich province with a relatively small population. The province's mix of English and French cultures and its proximity to the United States make it a unique destination for visitors looking to explore eastern Canada.

Haboku sansui or Haboku landscape is a style of Japanese painting that emerged during the Muromachi period (1336-1573). The term "Haboku" literally translates to "broken or splashed ink," while "sansui" means "mountain and water." Haboku sansui is also known as "ink-splashed landscapes," "splashed-ink painting," or "spontaneous brushwork landscapes." The Haboku sansui style is characterized by its use of rapid brushstrokes that create bold, energetic, and abstract compositions. Ironically, this seemingly chaotic technique was carefully planned and executed by the artist. Haboku sansui painters were skilled in the use of the brush, ink, and rice paper, which allowed them to control the flow and thickness of the ink. Haboku sansui paintings often depict a landscape scene (such as a mountain or waterfall) with fog or mist and incorporate the principles of Zen Buddhism. This style was influenced by Chinese literati painting and was intended to evoke a deep emotional response in the viewer, as well as blur the boundaries between the natural and spiritual worlds. One of the most well-known Haboku sansui painters was Sesshū Tōyō (1420-1506), who is credited with perfecting the technique. His famous work, "Winter Landscape," now housed at the Tokyo National Museum, showcases his mastery of the style and his ability to create a sense of dramatic tension and motion. Haboku sansui was highly influential in the development of modern Japanese art and had a profound impact on Western artists, including Vincent van Gogh and Claude Monet. Today, many contemporary Japanese artists continue to draw inspiration from this style, incorporating it into their own work as a means of expressing a personal and individualistic vision.

Kyle Casey is an American professional basketball player who was born on July 15, 1990, in Medway, Massachusetts. He is 6'6" and plays predominantly as a forward. Casey played college basketball at Harvard University, where he became one of the program's most successful players. Casey played for four years at Harvard from 2009 to 2013. In his senior year, he led the team to a school-record 26 wins and the Ivy League championship. He was named to the All-Ivy League first team and was also a finalist for the Jerry West Award, which is given to the top shooting guard in college basketball. After graduating from Harvard, Casey played professionally in Europe. He began his career with Fraport Skyliners Frankfurt of the German Bundesliga before moving to Italy to play for Pallacanestro Trieste. Casey then went to Spain to play for CB Breogan before moving back to Germany to play for Rasta Vechta. In 2018, Casey signed with the Melbourne United of the Australian National Basketball League (NBL). In his first season, he helped lead the team to the NBL championship, averaging 10.1 points, 4.9 rebounds, and 1.9 assists per game. He was named to the NBL All-First Team in the 2018-19 season. Casey has also played for the United States men's national basketball team in international play. He competed in the 2011 Summer Universiade held in China and helped lead the U.S. team to a gold medal. Off the court, Casey is known for his philanthropic work. He has worked with the NBA Players Association, the Harvard Square Homeless Shelter, and the Harvard Ed Portal. In 2020, he launched the Kyle Casey Foundation, which aims to empower student-athletes through education and mentorship. In conclusion, Kyle Casey is a successful basketball player, who has had an illustrious career in the United States and Europe. Casey is a philanthropist who strives to give back to the community, and his work both on and off the court is highly commendable.

The Velveteen Rabbit (or How Toys Become Real) is a British children's book written by Margery Williams (also known as Margery Williams Bianco) and illustrated by William Nicholson. It chronicles the story of a stuffed rabbit's desire to become real through the love of his owner. The book was first published in 1922 and has been republished many times since.

The Velveteen Rabbit was Williams' first children's book. It has been awarded the IRA/CBC Children's Choice award. Based on a 2007 online poll, the National Education Association voted the book #28 on the "Teachers' Top 100 Books for Children."

Plot summary 
A stuffed rabbit sewn from velveteen is given as a Christmas present to a small boy. The boy plays with his other new presents and forgets the velveteen rabbit for a time.  These presents are modern and mechanical, and they snub the old-fashioned velveteen rabbit. The wisest and oldest toy in the nursery, the Skin Horse, which was owned by the boy's uncle, tells the rabbit about toys magically becoming real due to love from children. The rabbit is awed by this idea; however, his chances of achieving this wish are slight.

One night, the boy's nanny gives the rabbit to the boy to sleep with, in place of a lost toy. The rabbit becomes the boy's favourite toy, enjoying picnics with him in the spring, and the boy regards the rabbit as real.  Time passes and the rabbit becomes shabbier, but happy.  It meets some real rabbits in the summer, and they learn that the velveteen rabbit cannot hop as they do, and they say that he is not a real rabbit.

One day, the boy comes down with scarlet fever, and the rabbit sits with him as he recovers.  The doctor orders that the boy should be taken to the seaside and that his room should be disinfected — all his books and toys burnt, including the velveteen rabbit.  The rabbit is bundled into a sack and left out in the garden overnight, where he reflects sadly on his life with his boy.  The toy rabbit cries, a real tear drops onto the ground, and a marvellous flower appears.  A fairy steps out of the flower and comforts the velveteen rabbit, introducing herself as the Nursery Magic Fairy.  She says that, because he has become real to the boy who truly loves him, she will take him away with her and "turn [him] into Real" to everyone.

The fairy takes the rabbit to the forest, where she meets the other rabbits and gives the velveteen rabbit a kiss.  The velveteen rabbit changes into a real rabbit and joins the other rabbits in the forest.  The next spring the rabbit returns to look at the boy, and the boy sees a resemblance to his old velveteen rabbit, and enjoys seeing the rabbit out in the wild.

Adaptations
The following adaptations have been made of The Velveteen Rabbit.
In 1973, LSB Productions made the classic, original 16 mm film version (running time: 19 minutes). It won the Chris Plaque Award, the Silver Plaque Award, and the Golden Babe Award, and it appeared at the Columbus Film Festival, the Chicago International Film Festival, and the Chicagoland Film Festival. It was a finalist at the American Film Festival. This film has been on TV and has been acclaimed by parents and teachers worldwide.
The 1976 Rankin/Bass television special The First Easter Rabbit adapts the plot of the book as first of part of its story of how a toy bunny is recruited as the Easter Bunny.
In 1984, it was part of the Enchanted Musical Playhouse series, where Marie Osmond played the part of the Velveteen Rabbit. Songs were composed by the Sherman Brothers.
The Velveteen Rabbit was adapted into a video recording and soundtrack in 1985 by Rabbit Ears Productions with Random House Video. It is narrated by Meryl Streep, with music by George Winston. It received a Parents' Choice Award for Multimedia and was nominated for a Grammy Award.
In 1985, two different animated adaptations were made at almost the same time:
The first, narrated by Christopher Plummer, was produced in Canada by Atkinson Film-Arts for CTV. This version aired in the United States on HBO.
The second was produced by Hanna-Barbera Australia and broadcast as an ABC Weekend Special.
In 1987, Don Bluth worked on an adaptation of The Velveteen Rabbit, to have been released by New Line Cinema. However, the project never was completed. Animation stills can be found online.
In 2003, it was adapted into a clay-animated film by Xyzoo Animation.
In 2007, a live-action short film adaptation was released by Horse Fly Studios. It was nominated for two Young Artist Awards.
Feature Films for Families, Family1 Films and  Believe Pictures released their live-action/animated adaptation in 2009. It is distributed by Anchor Bay Entertainment, Thomas Nelson and Vision Films. The film adaptation was illegally telemarketed to over 500,000 telephone subscribers in the United States.  Verizon successfully sued Feature Films For Families for the practice.
In 2014, Cat & Hutch produced a storytelling adaptation of The Velveteen Rabbit at Fulham Library for Hammersmith & Fulham ArtsFest. They then went on to perform this at various schools, libraries and other venues across London. In 2021, they adapted this version into an online storytelling over Zoom for various London libraries.
In October 2015, Atlantic Theater Company produced a new musical adaptation of The Velveteen Rabbit at The Linda Gross Theater. This production was adapted by Anya Saffir (book and lyrics) and Cormac Bluestone (book, lyrics, music).
In December 2018, Magic Light Pictures was reported to be adapting The Velveteen Rabbit as a Christmas special. It will be the company's first live action/animated production. Tom Bidwell was writing the scripts.
For many years ODC/Dance has performed in the San Francisco Bay Area a narrated contemporary dance adaptation of The Velveteen Rabbit with fanciful costumes, typically during the holiday season. 
The independent film production company Storybook Studio began production of a new animated film adaptation of the book, to be written and directed by John Patrick. The film will feature narration by Gabi Smedra and original music by Heather Huntington Stewart, starring child actor Peter F. Piccini as the Velveteen Rabbit, Maureen Russell as the Nursery Magic Fairy, Therese Kincadeand as Nana and Paul Fowles as the Skin Horse. The film will be released sometime during the year 2022, in honor the 100th anniversary of the original book's publication.
