Antoine Wehenkel is a Belgian computer scientist and professor at the University of Liège, Belgium. He is a world-renowned expert in the field of machine learning and intelligent systems. Wehenkel was born on October 14, 1958, in Liège, Belgium. He obtained a degree in Electrical Engineering from the University of Liège, Belgium, in 1982, a PhD in Applied Science in 1986, and a degree in Management from Solvay Business School, Belgium, in 1987. After completing his PhD, Wehenkel joined the University of Liège as a professor in the Department of Electrical Engineering and Computer Science, where he has been working ever since. He has also been a visiting professor at several universities in Europe and the United States. Wehenkel's research focuses on supervised and unsupervised machine learning algorithms, statistical signal processing, and dynamic systems modeling. He has developed novel algorithms for feature selection, clustering, classification, and regression that have been successfully applied in various domains, such as healthcare, energy, and telecommunications. Wehenkel has published over 300 papers in international journals and conferences and has received numerous awards for his contributions to the field of machine learning and intelligent systems. He is an elected member of the Royal Academy of Belgium and the Academy of Europe, and he has served on the editorial board of several prestigious journals. Wehenkel has also been involved in various industrial and governmental projects, serving as a consultant for companies and organizations in Belgium and abroad. He is a Fellow of the Institute of Electrical and Electronics Engineers (IEEE) and a Fellow of the International Association for Pattern Recognition (IAPR). In addition to his research activities, Wehenkel is also actively involved in teaching and mentoring students. He has supervised over 50 PhD theses and has taught courses on machine learning, intelligent systems, and optimization at undergraduate and graduate levels. Wehenkel is a visionary leader in the field of machine learning and intelligent systems, whose innovative research has contributed significantly to advancing the state-of-the-art and has had a significant impact on numerous applications.

You've Got This () is a 2020 Mexican film directed by Salvador Espinosa, written by Tiaré Scanda and Leonardo Zimbrón and starring Tato Alexander, Moisés Arizmendi and Fernando Becerril.

Plot
This is the story of Alejandro (Alex) and Cecilia (Ceci), a married couple living in Mexico.  Ceci is a driven career woman, focused on work and completely disinterested in having kids.  Alejandro wants children badly, and one day asks Ceci to agree to have a child as soon as possible, but she's not having it.  She's been offered a partnership in the firm she works at, which is a highly unusual promotion for a woman and is very excited about it.

Later that day, as Alejandro is walking down the street, he meets up with a waitress he promised to help if she needs it. She asks him to take her baby, Alan, for 3 days while she takes care of something important. A hesitant Alejandro agrees, and takes Alan.  He brings him home, trying to avoid having Ceci see Alan, but his plan fails and she does not react well. She gets quite angry and insists that he get the baby back to its mother as soon as possible.

The day Alan's mother is supposed to return comes and goes, so Alex sets out to find her. After a few more days, a friend alerts him that the mother has returned. He brings the baby back to her, and finds out she was being treated for brain cancer in the time she was away. She asks him to adopt the baby, but he refuses. As he begins to leave, she faints and, feeling bad for her, Alejandro brings her and the baby home. Ceci arrives at home just in time to hear him tell Alan's mother that she can stay with them.

They have a blowout and Alejandro breaks down, essentially admitting that the only reason he married Ceci is so she could have a baby with him.  She goes to a friend's house and they discuss Alejandro's insistence on being a father. He calls, and her friends convince her not to answer the call.

Over the next few days, he continues to try to call her over and over to no avail. Finally, she decides to call him and they connect very briefly, with Ceci asking him not to be home when she comes to get her things.

Alejandro makes plans to adopt the baby and move in with his mother. He heads to work with Alan, which is against office policy. He  is caught by his boss with the kid and immediately gets fired. The next morning, he goes into the office to apologize to his manager and has a conversation with her about how terrible she's been, making suggestions as to how she can improve how the workers regard her.  She rejects his suggestions, but says she'll consider rehiring him.

Ultimately, he ends up meeting up with Ceci at a major meeting she's attending for her promotion and they reconcile. As the meeting commences, Alejandro gets a call that Alan's mother has been admitted to the hospital.  Cecilia asks the CEO if she can present up-front and go to the hospital to support her husband, but he tells her it's just a formality and she's already been given the partnership/promotion.  All she needs to do is sign the papers.  She signs, and they head to the hospital.

Alan's mother dies, and Ceci and Alejandro adopt Alan.

The film closes with Cecilia packing her things to leave for Hong Kong, Alan sleeping on the bed nearby, and Alejandro joking about going to Hong Kong with her.  She gets upset, asking him not to joke because she'll miss him so much, and Alejandro pulls out plane tickets to Hong Kong, confirming that he and Alan will be joining her on her new venture.

Great Naval Battles: North Atlantic 1939–1943 was the first game in the Great Naval Battles series, published in 1992 for DOS.

Gameplay
Great Naval Battles: North Atlantic 1939–1943 was released in 1992 and depicts naval warfare in the North Atlantic during World War 2.

Reception
Computer Gaming Worlds reviewer in January 1993 stated that North Atlantic 1939–1943 was the first computer game to replicate "that 'joy of miniatures'". He cited several flaws in the gameplay but concluded that "for all the problems ... there are a half-dozen very nice features. For every minor disappointment there seem to be several glitzy positives. Frankly, I'm spending a lot of time playing it". A September 1993 review in the magazine of America in the Atlantic, Super Ships of the Atlantic, and Scenario Builder praised the latter's "infinite" replayability from the latter's random engagements, and stated that "SSI's efforts in refining and expanding this series are to be commended ... it has the potential of becoming the recognized placebo for practictioners of historical naval combat in this theater". A survey that month in the magazine of wargames gave North Atlantic, Super Ships of the Atlantic, America in the Atlantic, and Scenario Builder three-plus stars out of five. In April 1994 the magazine said that the CD version of North Atlantic 1939–1943s inclusion of all expansion disks and editors "made it a good buy for aspiring captains of the Bismarck", concluding that "It would be hard indeed to find a more comprehensive simulation of ship-to-ship naval warfare in WWII".

AIP Records was a record label based in Los Angeles, California. The label was founded in 1960 by the music entrepreneur Lou Adler and songwriter Herb Alpert. The name AIP Records stood for the record label's original name, Alpert's International Productions. The label focused on the surf rock sound that was popular in the early 1960s. During its lifespan, AIP Records released music from several notable artists such as Jan and Dean, The Surfaris, and The Hondells. AIP Records had a close association with the movie studio, American International Pictures (AIP), hence the name of the label. AIP produced a series of low-budget movies in the 1960s that featured popular music as an essential component of their films. AIP Records provided much of the music for AIP's beach and surf movies, which became the hallmark of the studio. Many of these soundtracks were produced by the label's founders Lou Adler and Herb Alpert. AIP Records had several hit recordings during the early 1960s. The label's most successful record was The Surfaris' single "Wipe Out," which reached No.2 in the US charts in 1963. The label also released the hit singles "Little Old Lady From Pasadena" by Jan and Dean and "Go Little Honda" by The Hondells, both of which reached the Top 10 in the US charts. By the mid-1960s, the popularity of surf rock began to wane, and the label's founders Lou Adler and Herb Alpert moved on to other ventures. AIP Records continued to release records until the early 1970s, primarily in the psychedelic rock genre. Today, AIP Records remains a nostalgic reminder of the golden era of surf rock and the movies that symbolized that era.

List of Australia national soccer team World Cup and Asian Cup squads The Australia national soccer team, also known as the Socceroos, has participated in numerous international tournaments. The team has competed in the FIFA World Cup and the AFC Asian Cup, among others. This article presents a list of Australia national soccer team World Cup and Asian Cup squads. FIFA World Cup Squads The Socceroos have qualified for the FIFA World Cup five times, with their first appearance being in 1974. The following is a list of the squads that represented Australia at each World Cup: - 1974 FIFA World Cup: Coach Rale Rasic selected a squad of 22 players, including Ray Baartz, Jim Fraser, and Johnny Warren. Australia finished the tournament without a win, drawing with Chile and losing to East Germany and West Germany. - 2006 FIFA World Cup: Under the guidance of coach Guus Hiddink, Australia reached the knockout stage for the first time in their history. The squad included stars such as Mark Viduka, Harry Kewell, and Tim Cahill. In the round of 16, Australia were eliminated by Italy after controversial decisions by the referee. - 2010 FIFA World Cup: Coached by Pim Verbeek, the Socceroos qualified for their second consecutive World Cup. The squad boasted players such as Lucas Neill, Brett Emerton, and Cahill. However, they failed to advance past the group stage. - 2014 FIFA World Cup: Ange Postecoglou led the Socceroos to their third World Cup appearance. The squad included young stars such as Mathew Leckie and Tom Rogic, as well as experienced players like Cahill and Neill. Australia finished bottom of their group, but impressed with their performances against the Netherlands and Chile. - 2018 FIFA World Cup: Under Bert van Marwijk, the Socceroos scraped through the playoffs to reach their fourth consecutive World Cup. The squad included the likes of Aaron Mooy, Mile Jedinak, and Daniel Arzani. However, Australia were knocked out in the group stage. AFC Asian Cup Squads Australia has also competed in the AFC Asian Cup on numerous occasions. The following is a list of the squads that represented Australia at each tournament: - 2007 AFC Asian Cup: Coached by Graham Arnold, the Socceroos won their first major international tournament by defeating Japan in the final. The squad included Viduka, Kewell, Cahill, and goalkeeper Mark Schwarzer. - 2011 AFC Asian Cup: Under the guidance of Holger Osieck, Australia reached the quarter-finals of the tournament. The squad contained many familiar faces from the 2006 and 2010 World Cup campaigns, as well as up-and-coming stars like Robbie Kruse and Matthew Spiranovic. - 2015 AFC Asian Cup: Postecoglou led the Socceroos to their second Asian Cup triumph. The squad included many of the players who had performed well at the 2014 World Cup, as well as newcomers such as Massimo Luongo and Trent Sainsbury. - 2019 AFC Asian Cup: Coached by Graham Arnold for a second time, Australia were knocked out in the quarter-finals by hosts the United Arab Emirates. The squad featured many of the players from the 2018 World Cup campaign, as well as new additions like Chris Ikonomidis and Awer Mabil. Conclusion The Australia national soccer team has a rich history of competing at international tournaments. This list highlights some of the players who have represented their country at the FIFA World Cup and AFC Asian Cup over the years. While the squad makeup may change from year to year, the pride and passion of the Socceroos remains constant.
